explanation: This bypass targets the 'OriginalFileName' and 'Image' path logic. By renaming a standard PowerShell executable to 'sqlps.exe' and running it from a non-standard directory, the 'Image' filter might trigger, but if the SIEM relies on the 'OriginalFileName' metadata field for the real 'sqlps.exe' (which is actually a SQL-specific shell), it will see the original filename as 'PowerShell.exe' instead, potentially evading the specific logic intended for the genuine SQL utility.

The rule specifically looks for the execution of the standalone 'sqlps.exe' binary. However, the functionality of sqlps is also available as a PowerShell module. By importing the 'sqlps' module directly into a standard PowerShell session, an attacker gains the same SQL-related capabilities without ever spawning the 'sqlps.exe' process, thereby failing to trigger the 'selection_image' or 'selection_parent' conditions.

output: New-Item -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\sqlps.exe" -Force; New-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\sqlps.exe" -Name "Debugger" -Value "C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe /c echo bypass" -PropertyType "String"
explanation: This is a persistence/execution bypass technique. By setting an Image File Execution Option (IFEO) for sqlps.exe, any attempt to call the binary results in the execution of a different 'debugger' process. While the rule detects the image, an advanced EDR/SIEM might be blinded if the process metadata is mangled or if the sub-processes are attributed differently depending on how the log source interprets the IFEO 'Debugger' handoff.
